advaita.bhashya_status: absent — Śaṅkarācārya's commentary on the Gītā begins at 2.10; no bhāṣya survives for 1.32
advaita.rendering: Arjuna declares to Kṛṣṇa: 'I desire neither victory (vijaya) nor kingship (rājya) nor pleasure (sukha). What is the use of sovereignty to us, Govinda? What is the use of enjoyments (bhoga) or even of life itself (jīvita)?' — Read from the Advaita vantage, this renunciation resonates with the disposition of vairāgya (dispassion) that Śaṅkara elsewhere treats as the necessary precondition for entry onto the jñāna-mārga. The very completeness of Arjuna's negation — rejecting vijaya, rājya, and jīvita together — gestures toward an intelligence that has seen through the structure of saṃsāric goal-pursuit, even though here that seeing is clouded by grief (śoka) rather than illumined by viveka. This rendering draws on the general Advaita framework rather than a bhāṣya on this verse.
advaita.voice_note: Inference from Advaita framework; no direct bhāṣya anchor for this verse.

viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_status: present — Rāmānuja's commentary on 1.32 is a sustained portrait of Arjuna's moral constitution and its collapse
viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Rāmānuja reads verse 1.32 inside a larger description of Pārtha as mahāmanāḥ (great-souled), paramakāruṇika (supremely compassionate), and dīrghabandhu (one of deep familial loyalty) — a man who had already been deceived repeatedly by the Kauravas in the lākṣāgṛha (house of lac) affair and yet bore no malice. Now, beholding kin about to be slain, his body drenched in sweat (atimātra-svinna-sarvagātra) from the combined force of bandhu-sneha (kinship-love) and karuṇā (compassion) and dharma-adharma-bhaya (fear of wrongdoing), he surrenders bow and arrows and sinks onto the chariot-floor. For Rāmānuja, this renunciation of vijaya, rājya, and sukha is not yet mature kainkarya (loving service to Bhagavān) — it is the painful dissolution of ego-investment in outcomes that, if redirected toward Bhagavān's feet, becomes the seed of bhakti-yoga.
viśiṣṭādvaita.voice_note: Anchored in Rāmānuja's bhāṣya, which narrates rather than glosses the verse word-by-word at this point.

dvaita.bhashya_status: absent — no Madhva bhāṣya text was supplied in the payload for this verse
dvaita.rendering: From the Dvaita standpoint, Arjuna's declaration 'na kāṅkṣe vijayam' (I do not desire victory) would be read as the moment when the jīva (individual soul), eternally distinct from and dependent upon Hari, exposes the bankruptcy of self-directed volition. Victory desired for one's own sake is āsuri-vṛtti (demoniac orientation); action undertaken as dāsya (servanthood) to Hari requires no personal desire-calculus at all. Arjuna's śoka-induced vairāgya is a half-truth: he has rightly negated self-interest but has not yet reached the proper re-anchoring of all action in Hari's will. This rendering draws on the general Dvaita framework; no direct bhāṣya anchor for this verse was supplied.
dvaita.voice_note: Inference from Dvaita framework; bhāṣya absent in payload.

śuddhādvaita.bhashya_status: absent — Vallabhācārya explicitly did not comment on verses 1.31–1.33
śuddhādvaita.rendering: Within the Puṣṭi-mārga of Vallabha, the question 'kiṃ no rājyena Govinda' (what is this kingdom to us, Govinda?) carries an inadvertent sweetness: Arjuna addresses Kṛṣṇa as Govinda (the lord who delights the earth, the cows, the senses) precisely at the moment he renounces all worldly objects. For Puṣṭi-mārga, the fruit of rājya is nothing; the fruit of rājya surrendered at Kṛṣṇa's feet as an offering within the divine līlā (play) is everything. Yet Arjuna in this verse has not reached that surrender — he has discarded the fruit but not yet placed himself inside the current of Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace). This rendering draws on the general Śuddhādvaita framework; Vallabha's own commentary on this verse is absent.
śuddhādvaita.voice_note: Inference from Puṣṭi-mārga framework; bhāṣya absent in payload.

bhakti.bhashya_status: present — Śrīdhara Svāmī's bhāṣya, stripped of HTML whitespace artifacts, is textually intact
bhakti.rendering: Śrīdhara's bhāṣya on 1.32 is tightly economical. He poses the opponent's question: 'Do you not see the fruits — vijaya and the rest?' — and has Arjuna reply with 'na kāṅkṣa iti' (I do not desire). He then expands: 'Those very persons for whose sake we desired rājya and the rest — those prāṇa-dhana-tyāgis (those who have renounced life and wealth) — have themselves taken their stand on the battlefield for the sake of this war. Therefore, what use have we for rājya and the like?' The logic is a complete collapse of the instrumental chain: the beneficiaries of victory have become instruments of the war itself; the goal-structure is self-annihilating, and thus Arjuna's refusal is not emotion but reasoned futility.
bhakti.voice_note: Anchored in Śrīdhara's bhāṣya; HTML artifacts (  spacing) stripped; Sanskrit content fully preserved.

advaita-bhakti.bhashya_status: present — Madhusūdana Sarasvatī provides the most philosophically elaborate bhāṣya of the six schools for this verse
advaita-bhakti.rendering: Madhusūdana begins by closing a possible objection: even if there is no adṛṣṭa-prayojana (unseen/merit-based purpose) for war, the dṛṣṭa-prayojanas (visible purposes) — vijaya, rājya, sukha — remain unimpeachable. Arjuna's reply is a motivational severing: phala-kāṅkṣā (desire for fruit) is what drives upāya-pravṛtti (engagement with means); without that desire, engagement is as senseless as a non-hungry man being urged to cook. Furthermore, the Govinda address is not incidental — Madhusūdana glosses 'go' as the indriyas (senses), making Govinda 'the one who presides over the senses as their constant witness'; by invoking this name, Arjuna signals that Kṛṣṇa already knows his inner vairāgya (dispassion) directly. Finally, the persons for whom rājya was worth desiring have themselves cast away prāṇa-āśā (hope of life) and dhana-āśā (hope of wealth) to stand in battle — making any claim of altruistic motive equally hollow.
advaita-bhakti.voice_note: Anchored throughout in Madhusūdana's bhāṣya; synthesizes the Advaita epistemology of motivation with bhakti's recognition of Kṛṣṇa as inner witness.

so_what_questions: When Arjuna says he does not desire vijaya (victory), rājya (kingship), or jīvita (life) — is this the speech of a liberated mind or a grief-struck one, and what would mark the difference?

Madhusūdana argues that phala-kāṅkṣā (desire for fruit) is the structural engine of all upāya-pravṛtti (means-engagement): if that is true, can action without desire be coherent at all, or does the Gītā's entire subsequent argument have to construct a different engine?

Śrīdhara shows the instrumental chain collapsing: the people for whom rājya was desired are now themselves instruments of the war. Does this make Arjuna's renunciation logically sound or does it expose a deeper confusion about the nature of obligation?

Rāmānuja presents Arjuna as mahāmanāḥ (great-souled) and paramakāruṇika (supremely compassionate) — virtues. Yet those virtues are precisely what disable him here. What does this say about the relationship between moral virtue and right action?

The address 'Govinda' recurs in the verse: Madhusūdana reads it as invoking Kṛṣṇa as inner witness of vairāgya. What changes if we read the address not as a philosophical signal but as an intimate cry — and do those two readings conflict?

From a Dvaita standpoint, Arjuna has correctly negated self-interest but has not yet re-anchored action in Hari's will. Is that re-anchoring a further step beyond vairāgya, or is mature vairāgya already identical with dāsya (servanthood)?

If the Puṣṭi-mārga reading is right that surrendering rājya as an offering within Kṛṣṇa's līlā transforms the act — what is the precise moment at which a renunciation becomes a gift rather than a collapse?

advaita.application: When every instrumental goal you have set — the project, the title, the relationship, the outcome — suddenly appears to depend on sacrificing the very people it was meant to serve, pause before acting. The Advaita pointer in this verse is not to give up, but to use the collapse of goal-structure as an invitation to ask: whose wanting was this? Sitting with that question — rather than immediately replacing the old goal with a new one — is the daily practice of viveka (discrimination) that Śaṅkara treats as preparation for genuine insight.
advaita.anchor: Advaita framework; bhāṣya for this verse absent.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Rāmānuja's Arjuna is praiseworthy precisely because he feels bandhu-sneha (kinship-love) and karuṇā (compassion) so deeply that he cannot act mechanically. In relationships where you must choose between institutional duty and care for the person in front of you, the Viśiṣṭādvaita counsel is: do not suppress the compassion — let it be felt fully, then ask whether that compassion is being offered upward toward Bhagavān or merely cycling inward as paralysis. The sweat on every limb is not weakness; it is the cost of moral seriousness.
viśiṣṭādvaita.anchor: Anchored in Rāmānuja's bhāṣya portrait of Arjuna's moral constitution.

dvaita.application: When you catch yourself refusing to act because you see no personal gain, examine carefully: is this genuine disinterest or is it disguised self-pity? The Dvaita teaching is that neither self-interest nor self-rejection is sufficient — the operative question is always 'whose project is this?' If you cannot identify any authority beyond yourself whose command this action serves, the renunciation is as ego-driven as the ambition it replaced.
dvaita.anchor: Dvaita framework; bhāṣya for this verse absent.

śuddhādvaita.application: Vallabha's Puṣṭi-mārga teaches that every object you release creates space in which Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace) can arrive. But the release must be directed — not merely a dropping of the bag, but a placing of it at Kṛṣṇa's feet. In daily life: when a plan you loved falls apart, the Puṣṭi-mārga practice is a conscious offering — 'this was yours to give or withdraw' — rather than simply feeling bereft. The difference between renunciation and surrender is whether there is a recipient.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Śuddhādvaita / Puṣṭi-mārga framework; bhāṣya for this verse absent.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's point is structurally precise: when the people you are working for have themselves entered the fire — when the colleague you were protecting has already resigned, when the child you were sacrificing for has already left — the purpose-chain breaks, and continuing in the old role is no longer service but habit. The everyday application is a willingness to re-examine whether your current effort is still genuinely serving anyone, or whether it is serving a story about yourself as someone who serves.
bhakti.anchor: Anchored in Śrīdhara's bhāṣya on the collapse of the instrumental purpose-chain.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's analysis of phala-kāṅkṣā (desire for fruit) as the engine of action offers a practical diagnostic: before beginning any significant undertaking, ask honestly whether there is a fruit you actually want — and if you find none, do not conclude you are therefore spiritually advanced. Absence of desire can mean liberation; it can also mean depression. The Govinda address in the verse is the key: Kṛṣṇa as inner witness of the indriyas (senses) already knows which it is. The practice is to bring that same unflinching inner witnessing to your own motivational states before assuming the action is pointless.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Anchored in Madhusūdana's bhāṣya on phala-kāṅkṣā and the Govinda-as-indriya-witness gloss.

self_assessment.bhashya_coverage: 2 of 6 schools have direct bhāṣya for this verse (Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana Sarasvatī). Rāmānuja provides contextual bhāṣya that narrates the verse rather than glossing it word-by-word. 3 schools (Śaṅkara, Madhva, Vallabha) have no bhāṣya in the payload for this verse; all three are flagged explicitly and rendered from school-framework inference only.
self_assessment.html_handling: Śrīdhara payload contained HTML whitespace entities ( ) in the framing text; Sanskrit content was intact and used directly. No Sanskrit was discarded.
self_assessment.polysemy_preserved: Key terms left in Sanskrit with parenthetical gloss: vijaya (victory), rājya (kingship), sukha (pleasure/happiness), jīvita (life), bhoga (enjoyments), vairāgya (dispassion), kāṅkṣā (desire), phala (fruit), upāya (means), kainkarya (loving service), dāsya (servanthood), prasāda (grace), bandhu-sneha (kinship-love), karuṇā (compassion), jīva (individual soul), mahāmanāḥ (great-souled), paramakāruṇika (supremely compassionate). No English suffixes attached to Sanskrit roots.
self_assessment.divergence_note: The six renderings track genuine doctrinal divergence: Madhusūdana provides a motivational-logic argument; Śrīdhara a purpose-chain collapse argument; Rāmānuja a moral-constitution portrait; Advaita points to vairāgya as jñāna-precondition; Dvaita identifies the missing re-anchoring in Hari; Puṣṭi-mārga distinguishes renunciation from directed surrender.
